clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of drooping of both eyelids that worsens towards the end of the day and difficulty chewing her food at dinner. She has no pain. On examination, she has bilateral ptosis that worsens after sustained upward gaze for 60 seconds, and mild facial muscle weakness. Reflexes are intact. After intramuscular injection of edrophonium, her ptosis resolves transiently. Which antibody is most likely to be positive in this patient?
explanation: This patient has classic myasthenia gravis (MG): fatigable ptosis, ocular and bulbar weakness worsening with use, and a positive edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Anti-AChR antibodies are present in approximately 85% of generalised MG cases and are the first-line serological test. The fatigability (worsening with sustained activity) is the hallmark clinical clue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anti-VGCC antibodies are associated with Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS), where weakness improves transiently with repeated activity and reflexes are depressed — the opposite pattern to MG.
Anti-AChR antibodies are the correct answer: present in ~85% of generalised MG and directly impair neuromuscular transmission by blocking and degrading acetylcholine receptors.

Anti-MuSK antibodies are found in approximately 40% of seronegative MG cases (those negative for AChR antibodies) and tend to present with prominent bulbar, facial, and respiratory involvement — a second-line test.
Anti-GM1 antibodies are associated with multifocal motor neuropathy and some variants of Guillain-Barré syndrome, not myasthenia gravis.
Anti-dsDNA antibodies are a marker for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), not myasthenia gravis.
clinicalPearl: Fatigable ptosis worsening with sustained gaze and resolving with edrophonium is pathognomonic of myasthenia gravis; anti-AChR antibody is the key serological test.
keyClue: Ptosis worsening toward end of day and after sustained upward gaze, reversed by edrophonium.
commonExamTrap: Confusing MG (fatigable weakness, normal or brisk reflexes) with Lambert-Eaton syndrome (reflexes depressed, weakness improves briefly with repeated activity, VGCC antibodies).
missedConcept: The distinction between pre-synaptic (LEMS, botulism) and post-synaptic (MG) neuromuscular junction disorders and their respective antibodies.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with known myasthenia gravis is brought to the emergency department with worsening dysphagia, dysarthria, and progressive dyspnoea over 12 hours. She had a recent chest infection for which her GP started amoxicillin-clavulanate. On examination, she is anxious, using accessory muscles of respiration, oxygen saturation 91% on room air, respiratory rate 28/min, bilateral ptosis, and nasal speech. Her forced vital capacity (FVC) is 900 mL. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent intubation and mechanical ventilation
explanation: This is a myasthenic crisis: acute respiratory failure in a myasthenia gravis patient with FVC <1 L (a key threshold for intubation), oxygen desaturation, and accessory muscle use. The immediate priority is airway protection and mechanical ventilation. IVIg or plasmapheresis are definitive treatments but take days to work; stabilising the airway comes first following ABCDE principles.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing pyridostigmine is dangerous — this may be a cholinergic crisis (excess acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) precipitating the deterioration, and increasing the dose risks worsening the crisis; in any case, airway takes priority.
Intravenous neostigmine also risks worsening cholinergic excess and does not address imminent respiratory failure; airway management is the first priority.

Urgent intubation and mechanical ventilation is correct: FVC <1 L, O2 sat 91%, RR 28, and accessory muscle use indicate imminent respiratory failure requiring immediate airway protection before any immunotherapy.
IV methylprednisolone can transiently worsen MG in the acute phase and does not address the immediate life-threatening respiratory failure.
IVIg is a definitive treatment for myasthenic crisis but takes 3–5 days to work; it should be started after securing the airway, not before.
clinicalPearl: In myasthenic crisis, FVC <1 L (or <15–20 mL/kg) is the threshold for intubation — secure the airway first, then start IVIg or plasmapheresis.
keyClue: FVC 900 mL, O2 saturation 91%, accessory muscle use in a known MG patient.
commonExamTrap: Starting IVIg without first securing the airway — IVIg takes days to act and does not prevent imminent respiratory arrest.
missedConcept: Myasthenic crisis is a medical emergency where airway and breathing take absolute priority before immunotherapy.
redFlagReason: Myasthenic crisis with impending respiratory failure (FVC <1 L, SpO2 91%) — failure to intubate will result in respiratory arrest and death.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old boy is brought by his mother who has noticed that he falls frequently, has difficulty climbing stairs, and rises from the floor by pushing on his thighs with his hands. She reports he was a late walker. On examination, he has pseudohypertrophy of the calves and proximal lower limb weakness. Serum creatine kinase (CK) is 18,000 U/L. His 7-year-old sister is unaffected. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This is a classic presentation of Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD): X-linked recessive disorder presenting in boys aged 3–5 years with proximal muscle weakness (Gowers' sign — rising from the floor by pushing on thighs), pseudohypertrophic calves, and grossly elevated CK (10–200× normal). DMD results from complete absence of dystrophin due to a frameshift mutation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Becker muscular dystrophy (BMD) is allelic to DMD but presents later (teens/early adulthood), follows a milder course, and CK may be less dramatically elevated — the onset at age 5 with very high CK favours DMD.
Duchenne muscular dystrophy is correct: onset before 5, Gowers' sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, CK >10,000 U/L in a young boy is the textbook presentation.
Spinal muscular atrophy type III (Kugelberg-Welander) presents with proximal weakness but CK is usually normal to mildly elevated, and fasciculations may be present; CK of 18,000 is not consistent.
Congenital myopathies typically present at birth or infancy with hypotonia and have normal or only mildly elevated CK; they do not cause calf pseudohypertrophy.

Juvenile dermatomyositis causes proximal weakness with skin rash (heliotrope, Gottron's papules), elevated CK, but the absence of rash and the extreme CK elevation with Gowers' sign makes DMD far more likely.
clinicalPearl: Gowers' sign (rising from floor by walking hands up legs) + calf pseudohypertrophy + CK >10× normal in a boy under 5 = Duchenne muscular dystrophy until proven otherwise.
keyClue: 5-year-old boy with Gowers' manoeuvre, calf pseudohypertrophy, and CK 18,000 U/L.
commonExamTrap: Confusing DMD with Becker MD — Becker is later onset, milder, and patients are ambulant into adult life; DMD patients typically lose ambulation by age 12.
missedConcept: DMD is caused by frameshift mutations in the dystrophin gene leading to absent dystrophin; Becker MD has in-frame mutations preserving partial function.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male university student is brought to the emergency department by his housemates with a 6-hour history of severe headache, fever (39.8°C), photophobia, and neck stiffness. He is confused (GCS 13/15). On examination, there is a non-blanching petechial rash on his legs and trunk. Blood pressure is 95/60 mmHg. Kernig's sign is positive. Lumbar puncture is planned. What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Perform lumbar puncture immediately to confirm diagnosis before starting antibiotics
Administer IV ceftriaxone 2 g immediately without waiting for LP results
Obtain blood cultures, then wait for CT head before any treatment
Start IV dexamethasone and wait for microbiology results
Administer IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg as first priority
explanation: This patient has signs of bacterial meningitis with meningococcal septicaemia (non-blanching petechial rash, hypotension, meningism, fever). In suspected bacterial meningitis, IV ceftriaxone must be given immediately — any delay in antibiotics significantly worsens mortality. The non-blanching rash indicates meningococcal disease which can progress to septic shock and death within hours. LP should not delay antibiotics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Performing LP before antibiotics is dangerous and wrong — in meningococcal meningitis with petechial rash and haemodynamic compromise, antibiotics must not be delayed. LP is contraindicated if there are signs of raised ICP or coagulopathy.
IV ceftriaxone 2 g immediately is the correct answer: never delay antibiotics in suspected bacterial meningitis — the non-blanching rash, fever, meningism, and hypotension demand immediate treatment.

Waiting for CT head before treatment is dangerous — antibiotics must not be delayed; CT is only needed if there are signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, focal neurology, GCS <8) and even then antibiotics come first.
Dexamethasone reduces inflammation but is an adjunct — it should be given with or just before antibiotics, not instead of them, and waiting for microbiology before starting treatment is fatal.

IV acyclovir is appropriate for herpes simplex encephalitis (temporal lobe involvement, seizures, personality change) — while it can be co-administered empirically, bacterial meningitis with meningococcal rash makes ceftriaxone the single most important immediate step.
clinicalPearl: In suspected bacterial meningitis, never delay antibiotics — give IV ceftriaxone immediately; the non-blanching petechial rash is a red flag for meningococcal disease requiring emergency treatment.
keyClue: Non-blanching petechial rash + fever + meningism + hypotension = meningococcal disease, give antibiotics NOW.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for LP or CT before starting antibiotics — this is a common and potentially fatal error; antibiotics always come first in suspected bacterial meningitis.
missedConcept: Every hour of delay in antibiotic therapy in bacterial meningitis increases mortality by approximately 30%; non-blanching rash is a red flag for meningococcaemia.
redFlagReason: Meningococcal meningitis/septicaemia with non-blanching rash and haemodynamic instability — delay in antibiotics is associated with rapid deterioration and death.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman is brought to hospital with a 4-day history of fever, headache, and behavioural change — her family reports she has been speaking inappropriately, aggressive, and confused. She has a history of one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure this morning. On examination, temperature 38.6°C, GCS 11/15, she is agitated and has mild expressive dysphasia. MRI brain shows T2 hyperintensity in the right temporal lobe and insular cortex. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: EEG showing periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs)
CSF PCR for Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV)
explanation: The clinical picture — fever, behavioural change, temporal lobe signs (personality change, dysphasia), seizure, and MRI T2/FLAIR signal in the temporal lobe — is classic for Herpes Simplex Encephalitis (HSE). The gold standard diagnostic test is CSF PCR for HSV-1/2, which has sensitivity >95% and specificity >99%. IV acyclovir should be started empirically before LP results return.
wrongAnswerExplanations: EEG showing PLEDs is supportive of HSE but is not the gold standard diagnostic test — it is a non-specific finding and cannot confirm the diagnosis; CSF PCR is definitive.
Blood cultures and procalcitonin are relevant for bacterial meningitis/sepsis but do not diagnose HSE; temporal lobe involvement on MRI and the clinical picture make HSE the diagnosis to confirm.
CSF PCR for HSV is the gold standard: sensitivity >95%, specificity >99%; it is the definitive confirmatory test for HSE and guides antiviral therapy.
CT angiography looks for vascular lesions (aneurysm, vasculitis) and is not indicated for this clinical picture; MRI has already identified the temporal lesion.

Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are relevant for autoimmune encephalitis (typically younger women, subacute psychiatric prodrome, movement disorder) which can mimic HSE, but fever and acute MRI temporal changes make HSE more likely; HSV PCR is the first diagnostic step.
clinicalPearl: Fever + temporal lobe signs (behavioural change, dysphasia, seizures) + MRI T2 temporal hyperintensity = HSE until proven otherwise — start IV acyclovir immediately and confirm with CSF HSV PCR.
keyClue: MRI T2 hyperintensity in right temporal lobe and insular cortex in a febrile patient with behavioural change and seizure.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for CSF PCR results before starting acyclovir — empirical IV acyclovir must be started immediately in suspected HSE; do not delay treatment.
missedConcept: HSE has a predilection for the temporal and frontal lobes; CSF HSV PCR is diagnostic and acyclovir must be started empirically before results.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man from Somalia presents with a 3-week history of progressive headache, low-grade fever, anorexia, and weight loss. He has a history of pulmonary tuberculosis 5 years ago, poorly treated. On examination, temperature 37.9°C, meningism, bilateral sixth nerve palsy, and papilloedema. CT head shows basal meningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture is performed. Which CSF profile is most consistent with tuberculous meningitis?
options: Turbid CSF, WBC 5000 (90% neutrophils), protein 0.4 g/L, glucose 3.5 mmol/L (serum 5.0)
Clear CSF, WBC 5 (all lymphocytes), protein 0.35 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (serum 5.0), oligoclonal bands
Xanthochromic CSF, WBC 500 (100% red cells), protein 1.2 g/L, glucose 3.8 mmol/L (serum 5.0)
Clear/turbid CSF, WBC 200 (90% lymphocytes), protein 2.0 g/L, glucose 1.5 mmol/L (serum 5.0), AFB smear positive
Clear CSF, WBC 50 (lymphocytes), protein 0.5 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L (serum 5.0), positive India ink
explanation: Tuberculous meningitis classically produces a lymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500 WBC), markedly elevated protein (1–5 g/L), and very low glucose (CSF:serum ratio <0.5) — often <2 mmol/L — with AFB on smear (sensitivity ~40%). Basal meningeal enhancement and cranial nerve palsies (especially VI) with hydrocephalus are classic radiological features. The chronically raised ICP with basilar involvement is hallmark.
wrongAnswerExplanations: High neutrophil pleocytosis with normal glucose and protein suggests acute bacterial meningitis (e.g. pneumococcal/meningococcal), not TBM which causes lymphocytic CSF with low glucose.
Normal glucose and protein with oligoclonal bands and minimal pleocytosis suggests multiple sclerosis or viral meningitis, not TBM; glucose is preserved and protein is not dramatically elevated.
Xanthochromic CSF with red cells suggests subarachnoid haemorrhage, not meningitis; the clinical picture of basilar meningeal enhancement and subacute course does not fit.

Lymphocytic pleocytosis, markedly elevated protein, very low glucose, and positive AFB is the classic TBM profile — this is the correct answer, matching the chronic presentation, basalar enhancement, sixth nerve palsy, and prior TB history.

India ink positivity identifies cryptococcal meningitis, which can mimic TBM; however, the prior TB history, basilar enhancement, and sixth nerve palsy with hydrocephalus favour TBM over cryptococcal disease.
clinicalPearl: TBM: lymphocytic pleocytosis + very low CSF glucose (<2 mmol/L or ratio <0.5) + high protein + basal meningeal enhancement + cranial nerve palsies = start quadruple anti-TB therapy immediately.
keyClue: 3-week history, prior TB, bilateral sixth nerve palsy, basal meningeal enhancement, hydrocephalus.
commonExamTrap: Confusing TBM (lymphocytic, low glucose, high protein) with viral meningitis (lymphocytic but normal glucose and protein) — TBM has very low glucose and markedly elevated protein.
missedConcept: TBM has the lowest CSF glucose of all meningitides; basal meningeal enhancement causing cranial nerve palsies (especially VI and III) is a radiological hallmark.

sourceLecture: Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord
sourceFile: 5 Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord.pdf
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with a 6-week history of low back pain presents acutely with sudden worsening of back pain radiating bilaterally to both legs, perineal numbness (saddle anaesthesia), difficulty initiating micturition, and recent onset faecal incontinence. On neurological examination, he has bilateral lower limb weakness (4/5 hip flexors and knee extensors), absent ankle reflexes bilaterally, and reduced perianal sensation. What is the most critical immediate management step?
options: Admit for urgent MRI spine and same-day neurosurgical review
Perform lumbar puncture to evaluate for Guillain-Barré syndrome
Arrange outpatient CT myelogram in 48 hours
Start high-dose oral corticosteroids and reassess in 24 hours
explanation: This is cauda equina syndrome (CES) — a surgical emergency characterised by saddle anaesthesia, bilateral lower limb weakness, absent reflexes, and bowel/bladder dysfunction. The standard of care is urgent MRI of the lumbar spine (the investigation of choice) followed by emergency surgical decompression, ideally within 24–48 hours. Delay beyond this window leads to permanent bowel/bladder dysfunction and paralysis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: NSAIDs and physiotherapy are completely inappropriate for cauda equina syndrome — this is a surgical emergency requiring urgent imaging and decompression; conservative management will result in permanent disability.

Urgent MRI spine and same-day neurosurgical review is the correct answer: CES is a neurosurgical emergency; MRI defines the level and cause of compression, and decompression must be performed urgently to preserve neurological function.
Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in suspected cord/cauda equina compression and does not provide the anatomical information needed — MRI is the investigation of choice.
Outpatient CT myelogram in 48 hours introduces an unacceptable delay; every hour without decompression risks permanent loss of bladder, bowel, and sexual function.
High-dose corticosteroids do not decompress mechanical cauda equina compression from a disc prolapse or tumour; surgical decompression is required.
clinicalPearl: Saddle anaesthesia + bilateral leg weakness + absent ankle reflexes + bladder/bowel dysfunction = cauda equina syndrome — surgical emergency requiring immediate MRI and same-day neurosurgical review.
keyClue: Saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, absent ankle reflexes, urinary retention, and faecal incontinence.
commonExamTrap: Treating as simple disc prolapse and prescribing analgesia — the combination of bilateral symptoms, saddle anaesthesia, and sphincter dysfunction defines cauda equina syndrome which mandates immediate imaging and surgery.
missedConcept: Cauda equina syndrome involves compression of the nerve roots below L1; the classic triad is saddle anaesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction, and lower motor neuron leg weakness — this is a surgical emergency.
redFlagReason: Cauda equina syndrome is a neurosurgical emergency — delay in decompression beyond 24–48 hours causes permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction.

sourceLecture: Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord
sourceFile: 5 Disorders Of The Spine & Spinal Cord.pdf
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of gradually worsening weakness and wasting of the small muscles of both hands. He has noticed that he frequently burns his hands without feeling the pain. On examination, there is wasting of the intrinsic hand muscles bilaterally, cape-like loss of pain and temperature sensation across both shoulders, upper arms, and chest with preserved light touch and joint position sense, and absent deep tendon reflexes in the upper limbs with preserved lower limb reflexes. MRI demonstrates a fluid-filled cavity within the spinal cord at C4–T2. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord
explanation: Syringomyelia is a fluid-filled cavity (syrinx) within the spinal cord that expands and damages crossing spinothalamic fibres first, producing dissociated sensory loss — pain and temperature lost with preserved light touch/proprioception — in a cape-like (shawl) distribution. It causes lower motor neuron signs in the arms (weakness, wasting, absent reflexes) with cord compression causing UMN signs below. MRI confirming an intramedullary cyst is diagnostic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ALS involves both upper and lower motor neurons but does not cause dissociated sensory loss (sensory pathways are typically spared in ALS); the sensory dissociation here is characteristic of syringomyelia.

Cervical spondylotic myelopathy causes myelopathy with UMN signs in the legs, but dissociated sensory loss in a cape distribution is not typical; the MRI showing an intramedullary cyst confirms syringomyelia.
Syringomyelia is correct: dissociated sensory loss (spinothalamic affected, dorsal columns preserved) in a cape distribution + LMN signs in arms + MRI intramedullary cyst = classic syringomyelia.
Multiple sclerosis causes demyelinating lesions but would show plaques on MRI (not a syrinx), and the progressive symmetric LMN hand weakness does not fit a typical MS pattern.

Subacute combined degeneration affects the dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts (B12 deficiency), causing proprioception loss and UMN signs — not dissociated pain/temperature loss or LMN upper limb signs.
clinicalPearl: Cape-like dissociated sensory loss (pain/temperature lost, light touch/proprioception preserved) + LMN hand weakness + MRI intramedullary cyst = syringomyelia.
keyClue: Cape-distribution pain and temperature loss with preserved light touch/proprioception, hand wasting, and MRI intramedullary cyst.
commonExamTrap: Missing that dissociated sensory loss (spinothalamic spared, dorsal columns preserved) is the hallmark of syringomyelia due to central cord expansion damaging crossing fibres.
missedConcept: Syringomyelia expands centrally, damaging decussating spinothalamic fibres first, causing dissociated sensory loss; common associations include Chiari malformation, trauma, and tumour.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old hypertensive man presents with a 6-week history of progressive right-sided arm and leg weakness, word-finding difficulties, and new-onset headaches worse in the morning and when lying flat. His wife reports personality changes over the past month. On examination, BP 155/90 mmHg, GCS 14/15, right hemiparesis (MRC 3/5), and expressive dysphasia. MRI brain with gadolinium shows a large heterogeneous ring-enhancing lesion in the left frontal lobe with significant surrounding oedema and midline shift of 8 mm. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade IV astrocytoma) is the most common and aggressive primary brain tumour in adults. It classically presents in middle-aged to older adults with progressive focal neurological deficits, personality change, and raised ICP symptoms (morning headaches). MRI shows a heterogeneous ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis, surrounding oedema, and mass effect. It is the most likely diagnosis in a single ring-enhancing lesion in an immunocompetent older adult.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Brain abscess also produces ring enhancement but is typically associated with fever, immunocompromise, or a source of infection (sinusitis, dental, cardiac); the progressive 6-week course in an immunocompetent patient without infectious features favours GBM.

Primary CNS lymphoma is also ring-enhancing but more commonly occurs in immunocompromised patients (HIV, transplant), tends to be periventricular, and often responds dramatically to steroids; less likely in an immunocompetent 62-year-old.

Glioblastoma multiforme is correct: single heterogeneous ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis, progressive focal deficit, raised ICP symptoms, and personality change in an immunocompetent middle-aged adult is classic GBM.

Metastatic cerebral deposits are more common than GBM statistically, but typically present as multiple lesions at the grey-white junction; a single lesion without known primary malignancy in this context points to GBM.
Haemorrhagic stroke transformation would have acute onset, not 6-week progression; the pattern of ring enhancement and surrounding oedema on MRI with gadolinium is not consistent with stroke.
clinicalPearl: A single ring-enhancing brain lesion with central necrosis, mass effect, and progressive neurological decline in an immunocompetent adult over 50 = GBM until proven otherwise; histology (stereotactic biopsy) is required for definitive diagnosis.
keyClue: Progressive 6-week course, personality change, heterogeneous ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis and midline shift in a 62-year-old.
commonExamTrap: Attributing ring enhancement solely to brain abscess — GBM and metastases must always be considered; fever and infection source favour abscess, whereas progressive neurological deficit without infection favours GBM.
missedConcept: GBM (WHO grade IV glioma) is the most common malignant primary brain tumour in adults; ring enhancement reflects central necrosis due to rapid tumour growth outstripping blood supply.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman with known glioblastoma on dexamethasone is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. She was found unresponsive at home. On examination, GCS 6/15 (E1V2M3), BP 195/110 mmHg, heart rate 48 bpm, respiratory rate 6 and irregular. Right pupil is 7 mm and non-reactive; left pupil is 3 mm and reactive. There is right-sided extensor posturing. What does the right pupil finding indicate and what is the most urgent intervention?
options: Pontine haemorrhage; start IV labetalol for hypertension
Uncal herniation with right CN III compression; intubate and hyperventilate, give IV mannitol
Left hemisphere stroke; obtain urgent CT angiography
Metabolic encephalopathy; administer IV glucose and naloxone
Psychogenic unresponsiveness; perform cold caloric testing
explanation: This is uncal (transtentorial) herniation: rising ICP from the glioblastoma causes the uncus to herniate over the tentorium, compressing the ipsilateral CN III (fixed, dilated pupil), then the contralateral cerebral peduncle (ipsilateral extensor posturing paradoxically — Kernohan's notch). Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) confirms severe raised ICP. Emergency management: RSI intubation + hyperventilation (target PaCO2 30–35 mmHg) + IV mannitol 20% (0.25–1 g/kg) to temporise while arranging neurosurgery.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pontine haemorrhage causes bilateral pinpoint pupils (not unilateral dilation), absent horizontal gaze, and quadriplegia; unilateral fixed dilated pupil indicates CN III compression from uncal herniation, not pontine pathology.

Uncal herniation with CN III compression is correct: unilateral fixed dilated pupil + Cushing's triad + extensor posturing in a known brain tumour patient = transtentorial herniation; intubate, hyperventilate, and give mannitol immediately.
Acute stroke does not cause Cushing's triad or unilateral CN III palsy in this acute progression pattern; known GBM with rapid deterioration points to herniation, not a new stroke.
Metabolic encephalopathy causes bilateral miosis or normal pupils, not a unilateral fixed dilated pupil; there is no indication for glucose or naloxone here.
Psychogenic unresponsiveness cannot produce fixed anisocoria or Cushing's triad; this is a life-threatening neurological emergency.
clinicalPearl: Unilateral fixed dilated pupil + Cushing's triad (HTN, bradycardia, irregular breathing) + extensor posturing = uncal herniation — intubate, hyperventilate (PaCO2 30–35), and give IV mannitol immediately.
keyClue: Right pupil fixed and dilated (7 mm), Cushing's triad, extensor posturing in a known GBM patient.
commonExamTrap: Attributing unilateral dilated pupil to metabolic cause — a fixed, unilateral, dilated pupil is a CN III palsy from uncal herniation until proven otherwise; this is a distinct, rapidly fatal emergency.
missedConcept: Uncal herniation causes ipsilateral CN III palsy (fixed dilated pupil) before contralateral hemiplegia; Cushing's triad is a pre-terminal sign of brainstem compression.
redFlagReason: Uncal herniation with Cushing's triad is a neurosurgical emergency — without immediate airway management, osmotherapy, and neurosurgical intervention, death from brainstem compression is imminent.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old retired builder presents with an 18-month history of progressive difficulty walking, he describes his legs as 'stiff and heavy,' and frequent tripping. He has bilateral hand clumsiness and difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning his shirt. He denies bowel or bladder problems. On examination, he has bilateral upper limb wasting at C5–C6 myotomes, brisk biceps and knee jerks, bilateral Hoffman's signs, bilateral lower limb spasticity, clonus at both ankles, and upgoing plantars. MRI cervical spine shows multilevel disc degeneration with cord signal change at C4/5 and C5/6. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Physiotherapy and NSAIDs for 6 months before imaging
Long-term cervical collar and regular review
Surgical decompression (anterior cervical discectomy and fusion or laminectomy)
explanation: This patient has cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM) with established myelopathy (bilateral UMN signs in legs, LMN signs at the level of compression, Hoffman's sign, cord signal change on MRI). Progressive myelopathy with imaging-confirmed cord compression is an indication for surgical decompression — anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) or posterior laminectomy/laminoplasty — to prevent further neurological deterioration. Conservative treatment is appropriate only in mild, stable cases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Physiotherapy and NSAIDs are appropriate for cervical radiculopathy or axial neck pain without myelopathy; established progressive myelopathy with cord signal change requires surgical evaluation, not delayed conservative management.
Long-term cervical collar immobilisation is not a definitive treatment for progressive myelopathy; there is no evidence it reverses neurological deficits, and delay risks permanent disability.

Surgical decompression is correct: progressive myelopathy (bilateral UMN signs, bilateral LMN signs at compression level, cord signal change) requires decompression to halt progression and allow neurological recovery.

Methylprednisolone has no established role in cervical spondylotic myelopathy — it is used in acute traumatic spinal cord injury (and its benefit is controversial even there); it does not address mechanical cord compression.

Cervical traction is contraindicated in myelopathy — distraction of an already compromised cord can cause neurological deterioration; it is used only for specific radiculopathies under specialist supervision.
clinicalPearl: Progressive myelopathy from cervical spondylosis (UMN leg signs, LMN arm signs at level, Hoffman's, cord signal on MRI) requires surgical decompression — delay risks permanent cord damage.
keyClue: Bilateral UMN lower limb signs + LMN upper limb signs at C5–6 + Hoffman's signs + cord signal change on MRI = progressive CSM.
commonExamTrap: Treating CSM conservatively when myelopathy is established — physiotherapy is appropriate for radiculopathy or mild CSM but progressive myelopathy with cord signal change mandates surgical referral.
missedConcept: CSM is the most common cause of spinal cord dysfunction in adults over 55; UMN signs below and LMN signs at the level of compression is the neurological hallmark; progressive cases need surgery.

topic: Spinal cord tumour — ependymoma vs astrocytoma
clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman presents with a 14-month history of progressive bilateral lower limb weakness and numbness, worse below the waist, and urinary urgency with incomplete bladder emptying. She denies trauma or infection. On examination, she has spastic paraparesis (MRC 3/5 bilaterally), a sensory level at T6 bilaterally (loss of pain and temperature and light touch below T6), hyperreflexia at the knees, upgoing plantars, and a positive Lhermitte's sign. MRI spine with gadolinium shows a well-defined homogenously enhancing intramedullary lesion at T4–T6 with associated syrinx above and below the lesion. What is the most likely histological diagnosis?
explanation: Ependymoma is the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumour in adults (50–60% of intramedullary tumours). It arises from the ependymal lining of the central canal, is typically well-circumscribed, homogenously enhancing, centrally located, and frequently associated with a rostral and caudal syrinx ('capping'). The thoracic location and the associated syrinx above and below the lesion, with a well-defined homogenous appearance, is characteristic. Astrocytomas tend to be more diffuse and heterogeneous.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Meningioma is an extramedullary intradural tumour — it compresses the cord from outside, causing asymmetric symptoms and is seen as an enhancing lesion with a 'dural tail' sign; it does not present as an intramedullary lesion on MRI.

Spinal cord astrocytoma is the second most common intramedullary tumour (more common in children), but tends to be heterogeneous, eccentric, poorly defined, and ill-circumscribed; it less commonly produces a symmetric syrinx above and below.
Spinal cord ependymoma is correct: most common intramedullary tumour in adults, well-defined, central, homogenously enhancing, with rostral and caudal syrinx formation — classic appearance.

Spinal cord metastases are typically leptomeningeal or intramedullary focal lesions in patients with known systemic malignancy; there is no known primary here, and the well-defined enhancing central lesion with syrinx is not typical of metastasis.
Neurofibroma is an intradural extramedullary tumour (from nerve roots), causing radicular pain and asymmetric deficits; on MRI it appears as a nerve root lesion, not an intramedullary mass.
clinicalPearl: Well-defined, centrally located, homogenously enhancing intramedullary lesion with rostral and caudal syrinx in an adult = spinal ependymoma (most common intramedullary tumour in adults); astrocytomas are more common in children and tend to be heterogeneous.
keyClue: Well-defined homogenously enhancing intramedullary lesion at T4–T6 with syrinx above AND below the lesion in an adult.
commonExamTrap: Confusing ependymoma (most common intramedullary in adults, well-defined, central, syrinx) with astrocytoma (more common in children, diffuse, heterogeneous, eccentric).
missedConcept: Ependymomas are surgically resectable due to their well-defined margins (gross total resection is curative); astrocytomas infiltrate and are harder to resect completely.
